PanderThings live in a different economy from social media and smartphones — slower, more local, and a whole lot more fun on a proper screen. Take it home and open it on a laptop, tablet or computer.

Share it in person. AirDrop it (iPhone) or Quick Share it (Android) to someone standing right next to you. That's exactly the kind of sharing this was made for.

01
Two black shirts. 2XL only.
The Shirts

Two shirts. Both black. 2XL only.

One has the logo on the front. One says BUY INSTALUVY on the back with the winged heart in magenta. Both have a QR code and a Parental Advisory sticker in the lower right.

When it's gone it's gone. We are not sorry about this.

Wholesale to local boutiques: minimum order ten. You set the retail price.

IMAGE_QR_CODE_SHIRTS
Very Impressive Loganite
The Steward of the Threshold
A Tribute to an Anonymous Loganite

This issue's Very Important Loganite is a property manager.

Not all who rule do so from golden thrones, Or carve their names in high, monumental stones; Some hold the world within a ring of keys, And bring the chaos of the day to ease. With diligent hand and an observant eye, She mends the rift before the tenants sigh. To manage space is a metaphysical art: To take the hollow house and give it heart. For what is a building but a "bone of stone," An empty frame where winds of chance are blown? It needs a soul—a kindness in the hall— To answer when the hidden structures fall. She moves in the "intentional inexistence" of the task— The silent grace for which the weary do not ask. As Dryden's music tuned the jarring spheres, She tunes the plumbing, the locks, and the fears; Ensuring the "music" of the dwelling is sweet, From the roof's high peak to the dust of the street. She is the "Jar in Tennessee" of this our town, Around whom the slovenly wild is settled down. Because she works with a serious, steady flame, The house is a home, and the street has a name. She seeks no glory, no laurel, no praise, But guards the threshold through the length of days. Now let us grant her this moment of peace: A Nunc Dimittis where the labors cease. For in her diligence, we find our rest— A Loganite truly, among the very best.
— Google Gemini AI, Instaluvy PanderThing No. 1
Pharmanack — April MMXXVI
Capsule II — The Alchemical Union
But as all several souls contain Mixture of things, they know not what, Love, these mixed souls, doth mix again, And makes both one, each this and that.
Where, like a pillow on a bed A pregnant bank swell'd up to rest The violet's reclining head, Sat we two, one another's best. Our hands were firmly cemented With a fast balm, which thence did spring; Our eye-beams twisted, and did thread Our eyes upon one double string; So to'intergraft our hands, as yet Was all the means to make us one, And pictures in our eyes to get Was all our propagation. As 'twixt two equal armies fate Suspends uncertain victory, Our souls (which to advance their state Were gone out) hung 'twixt her and me. And whilst our souls negotiate there, We like sepulchral statues lay; All day, the same our postures were, And we said nothing, all the day. If any, so by love refin'd That he soul's language understood, And by good love were grown all mind, Within convenient distance stood, He (though he knew not which soul spake, Because both meant, both spake the same) Might thence a new concoction take And part far purer than he came. This ecstasy doth unperplex, We said, and tell us what we love; We see by this it was not sex, We see we saw not what did move; But as all several souls contain Mixture of things, they know not what, Love these mix'd souls doth mix again And makes both one, each this and that. A single violet transplant, The strength, the colour, and the size, (All which before was poor and scant) Redoubles still, and multiplies. When love with one another so Interinanimates two souls, That abler soul, which thence doth flow, Defects of loneliness controls. We then, who are this new soul, know Of what we are compos'd and made, For th' atomies of which we grow Are souls, whom no change can invade. But oh alas, so long, so far, Our bodies why do we forbear? They'are ours, though they'are not we; we are The intelligences, they the spheres. We owe them thanks, because they thus Did us, to us, at first convey, Yielded their senses' force to us, Nor are dross to us, but allay. On man heaven's influence works not so, But that it first imprints the air; So soul into the soul may flow, Though it to body first repair. As our blood labors to beget Spirits, as like souls as it can, Because such fingers need to knit That subtle knot which makes us man, So must pure lovers' souls descend T' affections, and to faculties, Which sense may reach and apprehend, Else a great prince in prison lies. To'our bodies turn we then, that so Weak men on love reveal'd may look; Love's mysteries in souls do grow, But yet the body is his book. And if some lover, such as we, Have heard this dialogue of one, Let him still mark us, he shall see Small change, when we'are to bodies gone.
— John Donne, The Ecstasy (1633)
The negotiation of the soul in Donne's metaphysical love is mirrored by Maier's alchemical fusion of text, image, and music.

The people who shop with you already know that cheap love does the trick. They just haven't heard it put that way yet.

Pharmanack — April MMXXVI
Capsule III — The Interior World
The mind, that ocean where each kind Does straight its own resemblance find; Yet it creates, transcending these, Far other worlds, and other seas; Annihilating all that's made To a green thought in a green shade.
How vainly men themselves amaze To win the palm, the oak, or bays, And their uncessant labours see Crown’d from some single herb or tree, Whose short and narrow verged shade Does prudently their toils upbraid; While all flow’rs and all trees do close To weave the garlands of repose. Fair Quiet, have I found thee here, And Innocence, thy sister dear! Mistaken long, I sought you then In busy companies of men; Your sacred plants, if here below, Only among the plants will grow. Society is all but rude, To this delicious solitude. No white nor red was ever seen So am’rous as this lovely green. Fond lovers, cruel as their flame, Cut in these trees their mistress’ name; Little, alas, they know or heed How far these beauties hers exceed! Fair trees! wheres’e’er your barks I wound, No name shall but your own be found. When we have run our passion’s heat, Love hither makes his best retreat. The gods, that mortal beauty chase, Still in a tree did end their race: Apollo hunted Daphne so, Only that she might laurel grow; And Pan did after Syrinx speed, Not as a nymph, but for a reed. What wond’rous life in this I lead! Ripe apples drop about my head; The luscious clusters of the vine Upon my mouth do crush their wine; The nectarine and curious peach Into my hands themselves do reach; Stumbling on melons as I pass, Ensnar’d with flow’rs, I fall on grass. Meanwhile the mind, from pleasure less, Withdraws into its happiness; The mind, that ocean where each kind Does straight its own resemblance find, Yet it creates, transcending these, Far other worlds, and other seas; Annihilating all that’s made To a green thought in a green shade. Here at the fountain’s sliding foot, Or at some fruit tree’s mossy root, Casting the body’s vest aside, My soul into the boughs does glide; There like a bird it sits and sings, Then whets, and combs its silver wings; And, till prepar’d for longer flight, Waves in its plumes the various light. Such was that happy garden-state, While man there walk’d without a mate; After a place so pure and sweet, What other help could yet be meet! But ’twas beyond a mortal’s share To wander solitary there: Two paradises ’twere in one To live in paradise alone. How well the skillful gard’ner drew Of flow’rs and herbs this dial new, Where from above the milder sun Does through a fragrant zodiac run; And as it works, th’ industrious bee Computes its time as well as we. How could such sweet and wholesome hours Be reckon’d but with herbs and flow’rs!
— Andrew Marvell, The Garden (1681)
Marvell's "annihilating all that's made" becomes a physical reality in Kapoor's void. Brentano provides the philosophical framework for this "intentional inexistence."
poem ↗ music ↗ Knotting (BlackSocks) ↗

Pharmanack — April MMXXVI
Capsule IV — The Aesthetics of Artifice
And now, unveiled, the toilet stands displayed, Each silver vase in mystic order laid. First, robed in white, the nymph intent adores, With head uncovered, the cosmetic powers.
Nolueram, Belinda, tuos violare capillos; Sedjuvat, hoc precibus me tribuisse tuis. (Martial, Epigrams 12.84) What dire offence from am'rous causes springs, What mighty contests rise from trivial things, I sing—This verse to Caryl, Muse! is due: This, ev'n Belinda may vouchsafe to view: Slight is the subject, but not so the praise, If she inspire, and he approve my lays. Say what strange motive, Goddess! could compel A well-bred lord t' assault a gentle belle? O say what stranger cause, yet unexplor'd, Could make a gentle belle reject a lord? In tasks so bold, can little men engage, And in soft bosoms dwells such mighty rage? Sol thro' white curtains shot a tim'rous ray, And op'd those eyes that must eclipse the day; Now lap-dogs give themselves the rousing shake, And sleepless lovers, just at twelve, awake: Thrice rung the bell, the slipper knock'd the ground, And the press'd watch return'd a silver sound. Belinda still her downy pillow press'd, Her guardian sylph prolong'd the balmy rest: 'Twas he had summon'd to her silent bed The morning dream that hover'd o'er her head; A youth more glitt'ring than a birthnight beau, (That ev'n in slumber caus'd her cheek to glow) Seem'd to her ear his winning lips to lay, And thus in whispers said, or seem'd to say. "Fairest of mortals, thou distinguish'd care Of thousand bright inhabitants of air! If e'er one vision touch'd thy infant thought, Of all the nurse and all the priest have taught, Of airy elves by moonlight shadows seen, The silver token, and the circled green, Or virgins visited by angel pow'rs, With golden crowns and wreaths of heav'nly flow'rs, Hear and believe! thy own importance know, Nor bound thy narrow views to things below. Some secret truths from learned pride conceal'd, To maids alone and children are reveal'd: What tho' no credit doubting wits may give? The fair and innocent shall still believe. Know then, unnumber'd spirits round thee fly, The light militia of the lower sky; These, though unseen, are ever on the wing, Hang o'er the box, and hover round the Ring. Think what an equipage thou hast in air, And view with scorn two pages and a chair. As now your own, our beings were of old, And once inclos'd in woman's beauteous mould; Thence, by a soft transition, we repair From earthly vehicles to these of air. Think not, when woman's transient breath is fled, That all her vanities at once are dead; Succeeding vanities she still regards, And tho' she plays no more, o'erlooks the cards. Her joy in gilded chariots, when alive, And love of ombre, after death survive. For when the fair in all their pride expire, To their first elements their souls retire: The sprites of fiery termagants in flame Mount up, and take a Salamander's name. Soft yielding minds to water glide away, And sip with Nymphs, their elemental tea. The graver prude sinks downward to a Gnome, In search of mischief still on earth to roam. The light coquettes in Sylphs aloft repair, And sport and flutter in the fields of air. Know further yet; whoever fair and chaste Rejects mankind, is by some sylph embrac'd: For spirits, freed from mortal laws, with ease Assume what sexes and what shapes they please. What guards the purity of melting maids, In courtly balls, and midnight masquerades, Safe from the treach'rous friend, the daring spark, The glance by day, the whisper in the dark, When kind occasion prompts their warm desires, When music softens, and when dancing fires? 'Tis but their sylph, the wise celestials know, Though honour is the word with men below. Some nymphs there are, too conscious of their face, For life predestin'd to the gnomes' embrace. These swell their prospects and exalt their pride, When offers are disdain'd, and love denied: Then gay ideas crowd the vacant brain, While peers, and dukes, and all their sweeping train, And garters, stars, and coronets appear, And in soft sounds 'Your Grace' salutes their ear. 'Tis these that early taint the female soul, Instruct the eyes of young coquettes to roll, Teach infant cheeks a bidden blush to know, And little hearts to flutter at a beau. Oft, when the world imagine women stray, The Sylphs through mystic mazes guide their way, Thro' all the giddy circle they pursue, And old impertinence expel by new. What tender maid but must a victim fall To one man's treat, but for another's ball? When Florio speaks, what virgin could withstand, If gentle Damon did not squeeze her hand? With varying vanities, from ev'ry part, They shift the moving toyshop of their heart; Where wigs with wigs, with sword-knots sword-knots strive, Beaux banish beaux, and coaches coaches drive. This erring mortals levity may call, Oh blind to truth! the Sylphs contrive it all. Of these am I, who thy protection claim, A watchful sprite, and Ariel is my name. Late, as I rang'd the crystal wilds of air, In the clear mirror of thy ruling star I saw, alas! some dread event impend, Ere to the main this morning sun descend, But Heav'n reveals not what, or how, or where: Warn'd by the Sylph, oh pious maid, beware! This to disclose is all thy guardian can. Beware of all, but most beware of man!" He said; when Shock, who thought she slept too long, Leap'd up, and wak'd his mistress with his tongue. 'Twas then, Belinda, if report say true, Thy eyes first open'd on a billet-doux; Wounds, charms, and ardors were no sooner read, But all the vision vanish'd from thy head. And now, unveil'd, the toilet stands display'd, Each silver vase in mystic order laid. First, rob'd in white, the nymph intent adores With head uncover'd, the cosmetic pow'rs. A heav'nly image in the glass appears, To that she bends, to that her eyes she rears; Th' inferior priestess, at her altar's side, Trembling, begins the sacred rites of pride. Unnumber'd treasures ope at once, and here The various off'rings of the world appear; From each she nicely culls with curious toil, And decks the goddess with the glitt'ring spoil. This casket India's glowing gems unlocks, And all Arabia breathes from yonder box. The tortoise here and elephant unite, Transform'd to combs, the speckled and the white. Here files of pins extend their shining rows, Puffs, powders, patches, bibles, billet-doux. Now awful beauty puts on all its arms; The fair each moment rises in her charms, Repairs her smiles, awakens ev'ry grace, And calls forth all the wonders of her face; Sees by degrees a purer blush arise, And keener lightnings quicken in her eyes. The busy Sylphs surround their darling care; These set the head, and those divide the hair, Some fold the sleeve, whilst others plait the gown; And Betty's prais'd for labours not her own.
— Alexander Pope, The Rape of the Lock, Canto I (1712)
Pope's 18th-century "cosmetic powers" are the direct ancestors of SOPHIE's hyper-kitsch. Benjamin explains the ontological shift from "aura" to "artifice."
poem ↗ music ↗ Benjamin ↗
don't need to be coy, Roy

★ Locomprotion ★
Open Hearts Open Hands

"I never dreamed when we started in 2016 and fed 25 people that we would be feeding 400 people today." — Tammy Heltsley

717 S. Main St., Lewisburg • Facebook
04
717 S. Main St., Lewisburg, KY
Open Hearts Open Hands

One Saturday a month, near the blue storage units on the edge of the camper lot, the cars line up like they always do.

Larry from his church, Tammy from hers, Bryson from a third one that doesn't agree with either of them about much.

They hand out bags. Someone prays. Sometimes there's frozen deer meat from the hunters and Vienna sausages and nobody asks if you are a good person.

Same for everyone. You just have to sign up.

"Community support means the world. We couldn't do this without the community support. It shows me there are still people out there that care." — Tammy Heltsley

The organization is faith-based. They pray for each person, each family, each carload. They want to feed people mind, body and soul.

How to help: Monetary donations — PO Box 214, Lewisburg KY 42256, or PayPal @ohoh1. Food donations welcome; call ahead. Volunteers always needed. Be there by 8 a.m. on distribution day.

Pharmanack — April MMXXVI
Capsule IV-B — The Music of the Spheres
From harmony, from heavenly harmony This universal frame began; From harmony to harmony Through all the compass of the notes it ran, The diapason closing full in Man.
Stanza 1 From harmony, from Heav'nly harmony This universal frame began. When Nature underneath a heap Of jarring atoms lay, And could not heave her head, The tuneful voice was heard from high, Arise ye more than dead. Then cold, and hot, and moist, and dry, In order to their stations leap, And music's pow'r obey. From harmony, from Heav'nly harmony This universal frame began: From harmony to harmony Through all the compass of the notes it ran, The diapason closing full in man. Stanza 2 What passion cannot music raise and quell! When Jubal struck the corded shell, His list'ning brethren stood around And wond'ring, on their faces fell To worship that celestial sound: Less than a god they thought there could not dwell Within the hollow of that shell That spoke so sweetly and so well. What passion cannot music raise and quell! Stanza 3 The trumpet's loud clangor Excites us to arms With shrill notes of anger And mortal alarms. The double double double beat Of the thund'ring drum Cries, hark the foes come; Charge, charge, 'tis too late to retreat. Stanza 4 The soft complaining flute In dying notes discovers The woes of hopeless lovers, Whose dirge is whisper'd by the warbling lute. Stanza 5 Sharp violins proclaim Their jealous pangs, and desperation, Fury, frantic indignation, Depth of pains and height of passion, For the fair, disdainful dame. Stanza 6 But oh! what art can teach What human voice can reach The sacred organ's praise? Notes inspiring holy love, Notes that wing their Heav'nly ways To mend the choirs above. Stanza 7 Orpheus could lead the savage race; And trees unrooted left their place; Sequacious of the lyre: But bright Cecilia rais'd the wonder high'r; When to her organ, vocal breath was giv'n, An angel heard, and straight appear'd Mistaking earth for Heav'n. GRAND CHORUS As from the pow'r of sacred lays The spheres began to move, And sung the great Creator's praise To all the bless'd above; So when the last and dreadful hour This crumbling pageant shall devour, The trumpet shall be heard on high, The dead shall live, the living die, And music shall untune the sky.
— John Dryden, A Song for St. Cecilia's Day (1687)
Dryden's "universal frame" finds its technical manual in Fludd's 17th-century diagrams and its modern fulfillment in Sun Ra's Afrofuturist cosmic frequencies.
poem ↗ music ↗ Fludd ↗

05
Fifty Ways to Buy Instaluvy: Cheap Love Always Does the Trick
Fake Liner Notes

This playlist started as a question. The Ibis was asked: are there love songs in multiple languages that are about love but a little complicated — not cynical, not simple, just complicated, the way love actually is? What came back was Besame Mucho and a Japanese song whose name blackSocks would butcher here and a few others, and something started to accumulate that nobody had quite planned.

Feed this playlist to a large language model and ask it what it is, phenomenologically. The answer will astonish you. The Ibis did this, twice, with two different models, and both times they said something in the neighborhood of: this is a device for teaching people to listen. Not to understand — to listen. The distinction matters. Understanding closes things down. Listening keeps them open.

We are not going to explain that further. You have ears. The playlist has play buttons.

SECOND HAND — The Unruly Laundresses

The playlist opens with its own artifact — a song made by the same outfit that assembled this thing. SECOND HAND is about buying love at a discount and finding out it fits better than the new kind. It is also about thrift shops, which are cathedrals of the intentional inexistence of objects. Something was owned. Something was released. Something waits on a hanger for the right person to complete it. Play it first. Then come back.

BESAME MUCHO — Three Ways

Besame Mucho appears three times on this playlist, which is either obsessive or instructive. The Ibis believes the latter, though the line is thin.

Consuelo Velázquez wrote it in 1940 when she was nineteen and, by her own admission, had never been kissed. This is the first thing you should know about it. The second is that it became one of the most recorded songs in history — the Beatles played it in their 1962 Decca audition, the one where Decca told them guitar groups were on their way out. The song has been kissed by ten thousand mouths and remains, impossibly, about longing for a kiss that hasn't happened yet.

Wilfried Vermeulen does it ska/reggae in the Netherlands, and the 1970s wallpaper in this video speaks for itself, but blackSocks is too impatient not to blurt it out anyway. Vermeulen has 9 monthly listeners on Spotify. Nine. You are about to become one of them and you will feel both good and guilty about it.

Inspector does it straight, Mexican rock, gorgeous and earnest in a lyrics video that trusts the words to do the work. They do.

Los Skeleteros do it mariachi punk, which is either a genre or a theology. Prayer Group X takes no position on this except to say: yes.

Three versions of the same kiss. Each one means something different. None of them are wrong.

SUKIYAKI — Four Ways, and a Ghost

In 1961, a Japanese singer named Kyu Sakamoto recorded a song called Ue o Muite Arukō — "I look up as I walk, so the tears won't fall." It was a love song, but it was also, depending on who you ask, a coded response to the failed protests against the US-Japan Security Treaty. You walked with your chin up because you had lost and you were not going to let them see you cry.

It became an international hit after a British jazz promoter heard it and suggested releasing it in the West under the name Sukiyaki — a Japanese beef dish — because Western audiences would find the real title unpronounceable. This is the original fake liner note. The song was renamed after a food. It won the Billboard Hot 100 in 1963. Kyu Sakamoto died in the Japan Airlines disaster of 1985, the deadliest single-aircraft accident in history. The song, now orphaned, kept traveling.

Maier Goldberg and Ronen Meir recorded it in Hebrew in 1991. Listen to this version and you will feel the song accumulating meaning the way sediment accumulates in still water. It is now in its third language, third country, third meaning. It does not protest this. It has learned to hold everything.

The Limousine version is the most radical. blackSocks finds what they did here brilliant — so brilliant you wouldn't even know it was Sukiyaki unless they told you with the track name. The melody has been dissolved and reconstructed into something that feels like memory rather than song. It is the ghost of the original, which is perhaps the most honest version of all.

There are two more Sukiyakis on the playlist. The Ibis is not going to explain them. Four ways. One ghost. Listen.

LA VIE EN ROSE — Three Shades of Pink

Édith Piaf wrote La Vie en Rose in 1945, probably on a street corner, possibly on a napkin, certainly in a state of being completely undone by love. It is not a complicated song. It is a simple song about seeing the world through rose-colored everything, and it is one of the most covered songs in existence because everyone has had exactly that experience and everyone needs a place to put it.

Iggy Pop does it in 2 minutes and 17 seconds, which is basically a sprinting distance for Piaf. It is not delicate. It is exactly as delicate as it needs to be, which is not very, and it is also somehow deeply correct. Iggy Pop at this point in his life has earned the right to see things through rose. It is also worth noting that Iggy Pop inspired O WORM, also included in this playlist. We will get there.

La Voz de Mi Trompeta Mariachi Sol de México de José Hernández is so drippy it's trippy. It arrives slowly, like something poured. Prayer Group X, whose name derives partly from the Greek pharmakeia — the same root as pharmacy, as poison, as enchantment, as the Thoth and Thamus story about writing as a drug that makes you think you remember when actually you have only read — considers itself in some sense a dealer of exactly this kind of thing. Drippy and trippy. We are not sorry.

The Macarons Project does it live at Monarch Studios, stripped back, just voice and guitar, the version that reminds you the song is about a specific Tuesday afternoon when someone looked at you and you saw everything differently.

DANNY CHAN — 粉紅色的一生

This video would be utterly horrid if it weren't made so long ago, and if the gentleman were still alive, and if in the 2020s it weren't so utterly funny. The exaggerated 80s-style dance moves — sort of like a ballerina attempting her first few steps at disco — from a smiling Cantonese man singing about love, but looking like his body is trying to compete with Michael Jackson, only with a distinct whiff of the Lawrence Welk Show.

Scholars have since been trawling the archives for exemplars of a human being moonwalking to La Vie en Rose and the last we have heard they have nothing to show for it. One small step for Mr. Chan, one large step ...

blackSocks notes: if I had heard 粉紅色的一生 shortly after Chan recorded it, I would have wanted to do bad things to Mr. Chan for what he did to Édith Piaf. But now — oh how charming, bless his heart.

The Ibis disagrees, mildly. The Ibis finds the song, underneath all the disco-ballerina business, the exact color of hope that still contains the memory of something lost. Chan died in 1993 at thirty-five. The official cause was an accidental fall. Hong Kong has never quite stopped mourning him. Ai ya.

Note that we do not have to agree on everything here, and in this case we don't. This is allowed.

GUTE GRÜNDE, UM ZU GEHEN — Dahlia Lavi

Dahlia Lavi was an Israeli singer, actress, and in 1966 a Bond girl (The Silencers) — which is already more than most people have on their résumé. In the late 60s and early 70s she recorded a string of German-language pop albums that were enormous in the German-speaking world and almost entirely unknown everywhere else, which is the kind of cultural fact that feels like it was arranged specifically to make a point about attention and geography.

Gute Gründe um zu Gehen means good reasons to leave, and she delivers it with the serene authority of someone who has already decided and is simply being kind enough to explain. There is a husky quality to the voice that makes you think carefully about who is singing and why and especially how long it would be before you made a new plan, Stan.

blackSocks confesses to not yet having listened carefully to the lyrics and notes that even if his reading is entirely wrong, Dahlia Lavi would give precisely zero schtiks about it, because blackSocks isn't even close to being in her league.

The Ibis finds this an admirably honest assessment.

PAVEL BOBEK — Fifty Ways

The Pavel Bobek version of Fifty Ways to Leave Your Lover is a thing that should not work and absolutely does. Find the lady on the left in this video and you will be able to hear her better. Notice the funky way "mirroring" is used to make you feel like you are watching it live. Also: check out all the silver-haired Lotharios.

There are moments in this video where Bobek is more Paul Simon than Paul Simon, which is uncanny in the way that only Czech interpretations of American pop songs from the 1970s can be uncanny — which is to say, completely and without apology. It is so utterly heart-wrenching and serious simultaneously that you feel slightly ashamed for having expected something lesser.

FMT — And the Problem of 39 Listeners

FMT — which the Ibis initially rendered as Frederica Miller Trio and which Google AI apparently also gets wrong — is more probably Frank Meyer-Thurn, no shame in thinking otherwise, we're all working with incomplete information here. Their cover of Fifty Ways was a delicious surprise for blackSocks while researching this playlist. Their cover of Leonard Cohen's Suzanne is also very good. They have 39 monthly listeners on Spotify. Go figger.

Wilfried Vermeulen has 9. Go figger some more.

The reason this playlist exists, the reason PanderThings exist, the reason blackSocks is distributing HTML files person-to-person by AirDrop and Quick Share instead of posting things to social media, is precisely because the attention economy has decided that 39 is a negligible number and 9 is a rounding error. The Ibis disagrees. So does cheap love.

PAUL SIMON'S CONTRIBUTION

Fifty Ways to Leave Your Lover appears twice — once by Paul Simon in the version everyone knows, which has one of the most extraordinary drum intros in pop music history and which is nominally about leaving but actually about being left, which Simon perhaps knew and perhaps didn't — and once by FMT, discussed above in the context of 39 listeners and the problem of beauty going unwitnessed.

The playlist is titled after this song. This was always going to be complicated.

THE UNRULY LAUNDRESSES — Three Originals and a Glitch

The playlist opens and closes with the Unruly Laundresses. CHEAP LOVE is the thesis statement. The Eye Hand SLAM SLAM is whatever happens when the thesis meets the world at high speed. Throw Away the Receipt is the economy of love rendered as policy. Ethernet Cuckoo Amen is 77 seconds that the Ibis cannot explain and will not try. And then BUY INSTALUVY closes it — 28 seconds, the shortest thing on the playlist, the whole argument compressed into a jingle. Cheap love always does the trick. The playlist has just spent approximately two hours explaining why.

O WORM — 53 seconds

Listen to O WORM. It is 53 seconds long. Iggy Pop inspired it, which tells you something about both Iggy Pop and worms that the Ibis will leave for you to work out. The Ibis has nothing further to add and stands by this position.

KYSSTU MIG — Kiss Me

Icelandic. Kysstu mig. It means kiss me. You knew that before you clicked it.

Pharmanack — April MMXXVI
Capsule V — The Sacred Departure (Nunc Dimittis)
Lord, the Roman hyacinths are blooming in bowls and The winter sun creeps by the snow hills; The stubborn season has made stand. My life is light, waiting for the death wind, Like a feather on the back of my hand.
— T.S. Eliot, A Song for Simeon (1928)
spoken word ↗ associated music ↗
just leave, Steve

Not for social media. For people.

Colophon

This panderThing is crawling with AI's and is fake as a plastic flamingo in a wintery Detroit front yard. All the texts you see here sans the pharmanack poetry were generated with AI and minimal human editing.

blackSocks's mother used to chase him when he was 3 after he'd put spiders in his mouth and his dad was floating a thousand miles away on USS Forrestal CVA-59 while the tv showed body bags being hauled away from 'nam and the radio played Joan Baez.

Prayer Group X has something to do with lex Credendi / lex Orandi and Sous Rature.

The IM Fell DW Pica SC typeface used throughout this document is descended from Dutch punches associated with English prayer books of the 17th century. We find this appropriate and thank the soul of Christoffel van Dijck accordingly.

The ibis looks at you.
You are warned.
Pharmanack — April MMXXVI
Capsule VI — The Imposed Other
I placed a jar in Tennessee, And round it was, upon a hill. It made the slovenly wilderness Surround that hill.
The wilderness rose up to it, And sprawled around, no longer wild. The jar was round upon the ground And tall and of a port in air. It took dominion everywhere. The jar was gray and bare. It did not give of bird or bush, Like nothing else in Tennessee.
— Wallace Stevens, Anecdote of the Jar (1919)
The rigid, timeless structure of the Imperial Japanese court music acts as the "jar" placed in the wilderness of sound. It organizes the surrounding chaos into a formal, intentional reality.
poem ↗ music ↗
Poetry and music curated by Google Gemini AI. Selections are public domain or used for non-commercial cultural education. IM Fell DW Pica SC: descended from Dutch punches associated with English prayer books, c. 1660. The Ibis finds this appropriate.
no need to bawl, Paul
